title: Cat Bleeding Inside the Eye (Hyphema): Causes and Emergency Care
meta_desc: If your cat has blood inside their eye, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn about Hyphema and why immediate veterinary care is essential.
content_html:
TL;DR: Blood inside the eye (hyphema) is a high-priority veterinary emergency that requires immediate medical attention to prevent permanent blindness and address potentially life-threatening underlying causes. Contact an emergency veterinarian immediately if you notice redness, cloudiness, or pooling blood in your pet's eye.

What does it mean if my cat has blood inside its eye?

Hyphema is the medical term for the presence of blood within the anterior chamber of the eye—the fluid-filled space between the cornea and the iris. Rather than a scratch on the surface, this bleeding occurs internally. You may see a small pool of red at the bottom of the eye, or in severe cases, the entire eye may appear dark red or cloudy.

Is blood in my cat's eye considered a medical emergency?

  • Yes, blood inside the eye is a high-priority emergency that is often painful for the pet.
  • If not treated immediately, this condition can lead to permanent glaucoma or total blindness.
  • Internal bleeding is frequently a symptom of serious underlying conditions, such as severe trauma, high blood pressure, or blood clotting disorders.
  • You should contact an emergency veterinarian right away to schedule an urgent evaluation.

How can a photo of my cat's eye help the veterinary team with triage?

  • A clear photo allows the triage technician to assess the volume of blood and check for signs of external trauma.
  • The veterinary team can observe the shape and responsiveness of the pupil to help determine the severity of the injury.
  • This information helps the clinic prepare for your arrival and determine the level of urgency relative to other patients.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, hyphema, or hemorrhage in the anterior chamber, can manifest as small focal blood clots, diffuse hemorrhage occluding deeper eye examination, or layered hemorrhage with older blood appearing darker. Common causes include uveitis, trauma, intraocular neoplasia, retinal detachments and tears, systemic hypertension, coagulation factor abnormalities, platelet disorders, hyperviscosity, and congenital ocular anomalies. Systemic diseases like feline infectious peritonitis, toxoplasmosis, feline immunodeficiency virus, and feline leukemia virus often present as anterior and posterior uveitis with potential intraocular hemorrhage, retinal detachment, and secondary glaucoma. Therapy is directed at controlling post-traumatic inflammation and intraocular hemorrhage.

Chapter: Ophthalmology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 497)

faqs.question: Can I wait until the morning to see my regular vet?
faqs.answer: No. Because internal bleeding can rapidly increase intraocular pressure and lead to irreversible blindness, your cat should be seen by an emergency veterinarian as soon as possible.

faqs.question: What are common causes of hyphema in cats?
faqs.answer: The most common causes include blunt force trauma, systemic hypertension (high blood pressure), eye tumors, or inflammatory diseases like uveitis.

faqs.question: Will my cat lose their eye?
faqs.answer: The prognosis depends on the cause and how quickly treatment begins. Early intervention increases the chances of saving the eye and restoring vision.
